Dear Mr, HalLabrin:
tsy letter of JuIy 13, L99O, I requested a Cornmission Opinion
concerning a proposed arnendrnent to the Redevelopnent plan for the
National City Downtown Redevelopment project. 
f was subsequently
advised that my request for an Opinion was rejected, but that rny
request \./ould be treated as a request for formal written advice.
On or about october 5, 1990, I received a written response to my
request, prepared by Legal Division counsel Bl-anca M. Breeze, (your
File No. r-90-s59 ) .
subsequent to receiving your response, I have discussed with Ms.
Breeze certain issues involved in the aforernentioned correspon-
dence. Our discussions, as well as discussions with the City
Council of National City, have prompted me to prepare this request
for reconsideration, In pursuing this request, we are concerned by
the far-reaching effects of the earlier written advice on the
participation of National City officials 
on the process of amending
the redevelopment plan for the National City Downtovrn Redevelopment
Project. 
We believe that based on additional inforrnation which is
submitted herein, and with consideratj,on of alternative methods of
dealing with the redevelopment ptan amendnent, the provisions of
the Pol-itical Reform Act can be accommodated in a manner less
irnpactive upon the participation of National City officials.
fn making this request, we ask that particular attention be focused
upon the issues of "bifurcationtr and the |tpublic generallyrl
exception, discussed in rnore detaiL belor.r.
I. 
FORESEEABILITY; BIFURCATTON
At pages 7-8 of the advice letter, Ms. Breeze states that "it
j,s reasonably fcreseeable that decisions concerning the
acquisition of additional land for the redevelopnent area will


February 8, 1991
Page 2
have a financial effect upon the value of the nunerous
interests in real property and busj,nesses owned by Mayor
Waters, Vice-Mayor Van Deventer, and Councilmembers Da11a and
Pruitt. . .'r (emphasis added) .
In reading this conclusion, it appears significant that the
proposed amendment to the redevelopment plan included an
element pertaining to adding additional Land to the project
area. 
Since the proposed redevelopment plan amendment
includes other actions which do not involve adding land to the
project area (e.S. designating ttacquisition areasrr within
existing project area boundaries), the question arises as to
whether these other actions could be considered separatel"y
(i.e. 'rbifurcatedrr) from the question of adding land to the
project area, so that the question as to whether an official
could participate in a decision would be dependent on the
particular action to be considered. It would appear appropri-
ate to consider the elements of the redevelopment plan on a
situation-by-s ituation basis, with the officiaL's decision to
participate in a particular element depending on the potential
effect of that elenent only on the official's 
financial
interests.
II.s@
At pages L2-r3 of the advice letter, there is a discussion of
the "public generally" exception. It is concluded that this
exception applies to Councilrnember Da11a Lrho owns a personal
residence within the redevelopnent area. 
It is further
concluded that the exception does not apply to Mayor llaters
and Councilnenbers Van Deventer and Pruitt because the facts
presented rrdo not indicate that a sufficient 
number of the
residents of the city own commercial real estate and business
interests in the redevel-opment area.rl
Since receiving the advice letter, I have requested informa-
tion frorn the city Planning and Finance Departments as to the

7l-2 commercial and industrial. buildings are located in

the redevelopment project area. A copy of the memorandum

fn order to further substantiate the number of business
